{"global":{"name":"Chen Huang"},"nav":{"home":"home","about":"about","research":"research","patents":"patents","publications":"publications","notes":"notes","gallery":"gallery","back-to-top":"back to top","random-post":"Random post","random-note":"Random note","tags":"Tags","institution":"Institution"},"home":{"greeting":"Hello, I'm","title":"Chen Huang","subtitle":"Exploring the Quantum World","resume":"Download Resume"},"about":{"title":"About Me","bio":"

Hi, I'm Chen, an incoming PhD student in Computer Science and Engineering at The Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK).

I am passionate about advancing quantum computing through both theoretical research and experimental realization. My academic journey has equipped me with a strong foundation in physics and hands-on experience with a variety of quantum systems, including neutral atoms, trapped ions, and semiconductor qubits.

I also work with quantum circuits. At BAQIS, my research focuses on developing efficient quantum compilation strategies that help bridge the gap between hardware capabilities and practical, scalable quantum computing.

","heroCard":{"default":"I am passionate about expanding my knowledge through interdisciplinary research and collaboration. If you are interested in discussing research or potential collaborations, feel free to reach out via email or connect with me on LinkedIn."},"nameMeaning":{"chen":"My given name is Chen, which means \"dawn\" or \"early morning\" in Chinese. This meaning suits me well, as I am a morning person who enjoys the calm and peacefulness of the early hours. Interestingly, \"Chen\" is sometimes misheard as \"Cheng\", which means \"orange\" in Chinese. Because of this playful similarity, my friends affectionately call me \"Cheng-Zi\".","huang":"My surname is Huang, which means \"yellow\" in Chinese. This name has deep historical and cultural roots. It is often associated with the Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure considered one of the founding ancestors of Chinese civilization."},"research":{"imperial":"I was an MSc student in the Controlled Quantum Dynamics Group at Imperial College London. My research focused on charge noise in semiconductor qubits. By conducting experiments and simulations, I aimed to understand the impact of charge noise on qubit performance and develop strategies to mitigate its effects.","baqis":"Simultaneously, I am also a remote research intern in the Quantum Operation System Group at the Beijing Academy of Quantum Information Sciences (BAQIS). My research focuses on quantum compilation with neutral atoms."},"news":["2026-05-21 A first-author paper was accepted by IEEE Transactions on Quantum Engineering (TQE)!","2026-02-24 A co-first-author paper was accepted by DAC 2026!","2026-02-02 I started my research assistantship at CUHK CSE.","2026-01-26 I received the official PhD offer from CUHK CSE. markdownRemark.excerpt: These are my notes for Analog Electronics, taught by Professor Tian-Ping Deng. We took this course online during the spring 2020 term, and…
markdownRemark.html:
These are my notes for Analog Electronics, taught by Professor Tian-Ping Deng. We took this course online during the spring 2020 term, and my notes are based on the HUST MOOC.

To be honest, I initially found this course to be my least favorite as an undergraduate. As a physics student, I prefer learning about fundamental principles, and this felt more like engineering. However, it turned out to be one of the most useful courses I've ever taken, proving essential to my postgraduate experimental research. I'm thankful I have these notes for a quick and easy review.

Download the full notes (PDF)

The notes, along with my digital electronics notes, were also posted on Zhihu, where they received over 5,000 likes and shares.

See the popular post on Zhihu

This is just one part of my HUST course notes collection. If you find this resource helpful, I'd appreciate a star on the GitHub repository!

View on GitHub
